What We Monitor / Low Voltage Motors

Asset type

Hundreds of motors. Zero visibility.

Most industrial sites operate 50–500 LV motors with no condition monitoring. ESA changes the economics: one installation at the motor control cabinet covers the entire fleet. Stator faults, rotor defects, and supply quality issues—detected continuously, from a location your electricians already access.

Talk to an engineer See what we detect

Why LV motors go unmonitored

The dark fleet: too many motors, too little budget

LV induction motors make up over 90% of the industrial motor population. They drive pumps, fans, compressors, and conveyors across every sector. Yet fewer than 30% have any form of continuous monitoring. The rest run until they fail.

70%+

of LV motor fleets have no continuous condition monitoring. They are invisible until they fail.

€5–50K

typical cost of an unplanned motor failure: repair, production loss, emergency response, and cascade damage.

50–70%

of motor failures originate in bearings. The remainder split across stator windings (20–30%), rotor faults (5–10%), and external causes.

How ESA monitors LV motors

Phase current is the signal. The motor is the sensor.

LV motors are ESA's native domain. Phase current measurement is direct—no scaling complexity, no coupling losses. Every fault that changes the motor's electrical or mechanical behaviour leaves a signature in the current waveform.

Electrical domain (direct detection)

Stator winding faults produce phase current asymmetry and negative sequence components. Broken rotor bars create characteristic sidebands at slip frequency. Supply quality issues—voltage unbalance, harmonics, single phasing—are measured directly at the MCC. These are ESA's highest-confidence fault categories on motors.

Mechanical domain (via torque modulation)

Misalignment, imbalance, and coupling wear produce periodic torque variations that modulate motor current at their fault frequencies. Eccentricity changes the air gap geometry, producing characteristic slot harmonic families. Bearing faults modulate current weakly—detectable only when producing rotor eccentricity.

Fleet economics

One SAM4 acquisition unit monitors up to 9 fixed-speed motors from a single MCC panel. Per-motor cost drops to £50–200 amortised. A technician installs 10–20 motors per day with no process interruption. This is what makes fleet-wide monitoring economically viable for the first time.

Energy as a secondary signal

ESA captures power consumption, power factor, and load profile continuously. Motors running below 25% load lose 10–15 percentage points of efficiency. Identifying 20 oversized motors in a 500-motor fleet can save €50–150K per year in energy costs alone.

What SAM4 detects on LV motors

Six fault categories from one sensor location

Confidence ratings reflect 18-month fleet performance data (Sep 2024 – Mar 2026) across 72 motor-specific condition indicators. “Conditional” means detection depends on operating conditions explained in the notes column.

Fault type What's happening How SAM4 detects it Typical lead time Confidence
Stator winding fault Turn-to-turn shorts, insulation breakdown from thermal cycling, contamination, or VFD voltage stress Phase current asymmetry and rising negative sequence current. Inter-turn shorts produce odd harmonic distortion. Park's vector method detects winding asymmetry 4–12 weeks Full
Broken rotor bars Cracked or broken bars in the squirrel cage from thermal fatigue or high starting currents Characteristic sidebands at (1±2s)f around supply frequency, where s is motor slip. Lower sideband typically larger. Most-researched ESA fault mode with 30+ years of literature 4–8 weeks Full
Supply quality issues Voltage unbalance, harmonic distortion, single phasing, or VFD-related interference. 1% voltage unbalance creates 6–10% current unbalance Voltage and current harmonic spectrum analysis, power factor deviation, and supply-synchronous patterns. Direct measurement at the MCC, independent of motor load Continuous Full
Eccentricity Air gap irregularity from rotor sag, bearing wear, or manufacturing tolerance creating non-uniform magnetic flux Rotor slot harmonic families specific to the motor's geometry. Static eccentricity produces slot harmonics; dynamic eccentricity adds shaft-speed sidebands. Less load-dependent than rotor bar detection 2–8 weeks Full
Misalignment & imbalance Shaft or coupling misalignment, rotor unbalance from thermal growth, installation error, or impeller deposit buildup 1× running speed component (unbalance) and 2× component (misalignment) in the current spectrum. Progressive increase as conditions degrade. Strong torque coupling to current signal 2–6 weeks Full
Motor bearing degradation Inner/outer race defects, rolling element wear, lubrication breakdown Bearing defect frequencies (BPFO, BPFI) modulate motor current weakly. Detection requires the bearing defect to produce rotor eccentricity (≥10–15% air gap reduction). Outer race defects are most detectable 4–12 weeks Conditional*

* Motor bearing detection is conditional: ESA detects bearing faults only when the defect produces rotor eccentricity. Bearing grease degradation, contamination, and early-stage spalling before eccentricity develops are not detectable via ESA. Vibration analysis detects these conditions 6–8 weeks earlier. For bearing-critical applications, pair ESA with vibration monitoring on priority assets.

Detection capability also depends on drive topology, sampling rate, load variation, and operating conditions. See ESA detection constraints.

Detection boundaries

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. These are the known limitations for ESA-based monitoring on low-voltage motors.

Motor bearing detection is conditional. ESA detects bearing faults only when the defect produces rotor eccentricity. Grease degradation, contamination, and thermal issues before eccentricity develops are not visible in the current signal. For bearing-critical motor populations, supplement ESA with vibration monitoring on priority assets.
VFD-driven motors. PWM switching noise complicates rotor bar sideband detection. Stator faults remain clearly visible. For the strongest rotor bar signal quality, prioritise direct-on-line (DOL) and soft-starter motors. VFD-driven motors still benefit from stator, supply quality, and eccentricity monitoring.
Low-load operation (<30% rated). Fault sidebands in the current signal are proportional to mechanical load. Below ~30% of rated load, rotor bar sidebands weaken and may fall below the detection threshold. Eccentricity detection is less load-dependent.
Small motors (<5 kW). Low current draw reduces fault sideband amplitude relative to the noise floor. Cast-aluminium rotors in small motors rarely develop broken bars, reducing ESA's strongest detection mode. SAM4 is optimised for motors from 5 kW upward.
Non-induction motor types. Permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSM) and synchronous reluctance motors (SynRM) have zero slip. The broken rotor bar detection model does not apply. Alternative fault signatures exist but field validation is limited.

Motor-specific data based on 72 condition indicators over 18 months (Sep 2024 – Mar 2026), ~97% detection accuracy on electrical faults. Zero misses on stator and rotor faults. See full detection transparency report →

Monitoring architecture

ESA and vibration are complementary, not competing

The question is not which technology to choose. It is which motors get which coverage. ESA covers the fleet. Vibration covers the bearings on critical assets. Together, they eliminate the monitoring gap.

ESA leads

  • Broken rotor bars and cracked end rings
  • Stator winding faults (inter-turn shorts, insulation degradation)
  • Supply quality (voltage unbalance, harmonics, single phasing)
  • VFD-induced winding stress
  • Fleet-wide screening at MCC cost
  • Energy and efficiency trending

Both detect

  • Eccentricity (static and dynamic)
  • Shaft misalignment
  • Rotor unbalance
  • Coupling wear and looseness

Vibration leads

  • Early bearing wear (6–8 week lead time advantage over ESA)
  • Bearing lubrication degradation
  • Structural resonance and soft foot
  • Very low speed motors (≤10 RPM)

Recommended three-tier approach

Tier 1 (top 5–10% by criticality): continuous ESA + continuous vibration + annual insulation testing. Maximum detection coverage and lead time. Tier 2 (next 30–40%): continuous ESA + quarterly vibration route. ESA provides the baseline; vibration confirms bearing concerns when flagged. Tier 3 (remaining 50–60%): ESA screening from the MCC. Turns invisible motors into monitored assets at a fraction of per-motor vibration sensor cost. This tier is where ESA creates the most value—it replaces run-to-failure, not vibration.

Motor detections

Real faults caught on LV motors

Imbalance Water

Motor imbalance in cooling pump

1× running speed component rise detected in the current signature. Rotating element imbalance corrected before bearing damage progressed.

Read detection story →
Eccentricity Chemicals

Rotor eccentricity in process pump motor

Air gap asymmetry signatures identified through rotor slot harmonic analysis. Rotor rub prevented through planned replacement.

Read detection story →
Winding fault Water

Stator winding degradation flagged on booster pump motor

Rising negative sequence current indicated inter-turn short developing. Motor replaced during planned outage. Zero misses on stator faults over 18 months.

Read detection story →
Misalignment Oil & Gas

Coupling misalignment on transfer pump motor

2× running speed rise flagged coupling misalignment caused by thermal growth. Corrected before bearing damage progressed to failure.

Read detection story →
See all detection stories →

Installation on LV motors

Under 60 minutes. No motor access required.

1

Open the motor control cabinet

SAM4 installs at the MCC: the same panel your electricians already access. No confined space entry, no scaffolding, no process shutdown.

2

Clip sensor onto motor supply cables

Current and voltage sensors clip directly onto the existing cabling. No wiring changes. No interruption to the motor's operation. One unit monitors up to 9 fixed-speed motors.

3

Connect and commission

The SAM4 gateway connects via cellular (4G/LTE). No dependency on your IT network. Monitoring starts immediately. First diagnostic results within 48 hours.

Full installation guide → Security & network architecture →
